sources.sourceName: Introduction to Sociology, OpenStax 3e (Pressbooks adaptation) - Appendix: Chapter Quiz Answers (odd-numbered)

meta.notes: Sociology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e Ch. 10 and Ch. 12 section-quiz pages; correct answers from the OpenStax-derived odd-numbered answer key (Pressbooks CC BY 4.0 adaptation). From Ch. 12, two True/False items (Q7, Q12) were excluded because they cannot be reformatted into four unambiguous MCAT choices, and Q17 (most-supported sex-ed program) was excluded because its keyed answer conflicts with what national survey research supports (avoiding a possibly-wrong answer). Live OpenStax page shows CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

hints.prompt: In sociology, describing a relationship as 'exploitation' most directly refers to what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One group unfairly benefiting at the expense of a less powerful group

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two groups trading goods for mutual benefit

choices.label: C
choices.text: Individuals sharing symbolic meanings in daily conversation

choices.label: D
choices.text: A society naturally returning to a stable balance

hints.rationale: Exploitation denotes a more powerful group benefiting at the expense of a less powerful one.

hints.prompt: The scenario involves powerful corporations benefiting at workers' expense. A functionalist would frame the same situation differently. What would a functionalist emphasize instead?

choices.label: A
choices.text: How the economic arrangement contributes to social order and serves a function for society

choices.label: B
choices.text: How the arrangement lets one group exploit and dominate another

choices.label: C
choices.text: The personal meanings individual workers attach to their jobs

choices.label: D
choices.text: How gender specifically shapes the workers' experiences

hints.rationale: Functionalism emphasizes stability and the functions arrangements serve, unlike a focus on exploitation.

hints.prompt: Which two of the main question's options can be ruled out first because they do NOT center on inequality or exploitation between groups?
hints.rationale: Functionalism stresses social stability and symbolic interactionism stresses micro-level meanings, so neither centers on group exploitation.

questions.stem: A sociologist who focuses on the way that multinational corporations headquartered in core nations exploit the local workers in their peripheral nation factories is using a _________ perspective to understand the global economy.
questions.explanation: Focusing on exploitation and inequality between powerful and less powerful groups reflects the conflict theory perspective.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 10 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q1 = B.

hints.prompt: Symbolic interactionism studies society primarily at which level of analysis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The micro level of individual, face-to-face interaction and meaning

choices.label: B
choices.text: The macro level of large institutions and structures

choices.label: C
choices.text: The global level of relations between nations

choices.label: D
choices.text: The biological level of genetic inheritance

hints.rationale: Symbolic interactionism operates at the micro level of individual interaction and meaning.

hints.prompt: Studying 'how inequality is created and reproduced' across a whole society is an example of which level of analysis, the opposite of symbolic interactionism's focus?
hints.rationale: Analyzing society-wide inequality is macro-level work, contrasting with symbolic interactionism's micro focus.

hints.prompt: Which two options describe MACRO-level or structural research questions and so would not be pursued by a symbolic interactionist?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'study how inequality is created and reproduced' and 'understand how companies advantage high-income nations'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'interview women about day-to-day life' and 'study how inequality is reproduced'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'improve the lives of low-income workers' and 'interview women about day-to-day life'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'advantage to high-income nations' and 'interview women about day-to-day life'

hints.rationale: Those two focus on society-wide structures and international comparisons, which are macro concerns rather than micro interaction.

questions.stem: A sociologist working from a symbolic interaction perspective would:

choices.label: A
choices.text: study how inequality is created and reproduced

choices.label: B
choices.text: study how corporations can improve the lives of their low-income workers

choices.label: C
choices.text: try to understand how companies provide an advantage to high-income nations compared to low-income nations

choices.label: D
choices.text: want to interview women working in factories to understand how they manage the expectations of their supervisors, make ends meet, and support their households on a day-to-day basis

questions.explanation: Symbolic interactionism works at the micro level, so a researcher would interview individuals to understand their day-to-day experiences and meanings.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 10 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q3 = D.

hints.prompt: In a business context, the term 'capital' refers primarily to what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Money, factories, and investment used to produce goods

choices.label: D
choices.text: The natural climate of a region

hints.rationale: Capital refers to financial and physical resources such as money, factories, and investment.

hints.prompt: The scenario involves relocating factories to another country, not replacing workers with machines. Replacing human workers with machines would instead be called what?
hints.rationale: Automation is replacing human labor with machines, which differs from relocating factories abroad.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be ruled out because they name a broad theory or condition rather than the specific economic action of moving factories abroad?
hints.rationale: 'Conflict theory' is a perspective and 'global inequality' is a condition, so neither names the specific act described.

questions.stem: In the past, the United States manufactured clothes. Many clothing corporations have shut down their U.S. factories and relocated to China. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Capital flight is the movement of capital (factories, investment, jobs) from one location to another, such as relocating clothing production from the U.S. to China.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 10 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q5 = D.

hints.prompt: Maya cannot reliably obtain food or shelter. Lacking the basic necessities for survival is a matter of meeting which kind of needs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Basic physical survival needs such as food and shelter

choices.label: D
choices.text: Needs defined only by comparison to neighbors

hints.rationale: Food and shelter are basic physical survival needs.

hints.prompt: Maya's situation is about lacking basic survival necessities, not about feeling worse off than others. Poverty measured by comparison to others' living standards is instead called what?
hints.rationale: Relative poverty is defined by comparison to the standards of others, unlike lacking basic necessities.

hints.prompt: Which two options are least appropriate, one because it describes a personal feeling of inadequacy and the other because it describes poverty at a worldwide scale?
hints.rationale: Subjective poverty is about perception and global poverty is a worldwide scale, so neither captures one child lacking basic necessities.

questions.stem: Maya is a twelve-year-old girl living in Thailand. She is homeless, and often does not know where she will sleep or when she will eat. We might say that Maya lives in _________ poverty.
questions.explanation: Absolute poverty is the inability to secure basic necessities like food and shelter, as in Maya's situation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 10 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q7 = B.

hints.prompt: Faith can afford basics but feels her income is inadequate for what she expected. This kind of judgment is based on a person's ______.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Actual lack of food and water

hints.rationale: Such judgments rest on a person's own perception and expectations rather than objective deprivation.

hints.prompt: Faith's feeling comes from her own expectations, not from an objective comparison of her resources to a fixed standard. Poverty defined by comparison to the living standards of others is called what?
hints.rationale: Relative poverty is defined by comparison to others' standards, distinct from a personal sense of inadequacy.

hints.prompt: Faith meets her basic needs. Which two options can be eliminated because they describe severe material deprivation or a worldwide scale rather than her situation?
hints.rationale: Global and extreme poverty involve worldwide scope or severe deprivation, which do not match someone who can afford basics.

questions.stem: Faith has a full-time job and two children. She has enough money for the basics and can pay her rent each month, but she feels that, with her education and experience, her income should be enough for her family to live much better than they do. Faith is experiencing:
questions.explanation: Subjective poverty is based on a person's perception that their resources are inadequate relative to their expectations, as with Faith's sense that she should be better off.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 10 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q9 = B.

hints.prompt: Dependency theory argues that poor (peripheral) nations remain poor primarily because of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Their ongoing exploitation by and dependence on wealthy core nations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Their lack of any natural resources

hints.rationale: Dependency theory attributes persistent poverty to exploitation by and dependence on core nations.

hints.prompt: Historically, how is China correctly described in dependency-theory terms before its economic rise?

choices.label: C
choices.text: As a nation with no economy

choices.label: D
choices.text: As a nation that never traded

hints.rationale: Before its rapid development China was a low-income, peripheral nation, so any option calling it previously high-income is factually off.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be ruled out because they state the opposite of China's actual economic history?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the option calling China a 'previously high-income nation' and the option saying China is 'growing more dependent on core nations'

choices.label: B
choices.text: the option about China 'successfully developing' and the option about China 'not wanting to be like core nations'

choices.label: C
choices.text: the option about China 'growing more dependent' and the option about China 'successfully developing'

choices.label: D
choices.text: the option calling China 'previously high-income' and the option about China 'successfully developing'

hints.rationale: China was not previously high-income and has grown less, not more, dependent, so both statements contradict its history.

questions.stem: One flaw in dependency theory is the unwillingness to recognize _______.

choices.label: A
choices.text: that previously low-income nations such as China have successfully developed their economies and can no longer be classified as dependent on core nations

choices.label: B
choices.text: that previously high-income nations such as China have been economically overpowered by low-income nations entering the global marketplace

choices.label: C
choices.text: that countries such as China are growing more dependent on core nations

choices.label: D
choices.text: that countries such as China do not necessarily want to be more like core nations

questions.explanation: Dependency theory struggles to explain cases like China, a once low-income nation that successfully developed and is no longer dependent on core nations.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 10 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: The stem says a nation develops when its people adopt values like hard work and embrace new technology. This view locates the main cause of development in what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A nation's internal culture and technological progress

hints.rationale: This view credits development to internal cultural values and technological advancement.

hints.prompt: The theory in the stem blames a nation's own culture and technology for its development level. A rival theory instead blames wealthy nations' exploitation. That rival theory is called what?
hints.rationale: Dependency theory attributes underdevelopment to exploitation by wealthy nations, contrasting with an internal-culture explanation.

hints.prompt: The stem credits internal culture and technology, not external exploitation. Which two options clearly belong to the 'dependency' family and so do not fit?
hints.rationale: Both name dependency-based theories focused on external exploitation, unlike the stem's internal-culture account.

questions.stem: If a sociologist says that nations evolve toward more advanced technology and more complex industry as their citizens learn cultural values that celebrate hard work and success, she is using _______ theory to study the global economy.
questions.explanation: Modernization theory argues that nations develop as they adopt cultural values and technologies associated with industrialization and achievement.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 10 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q13 = A.

hints.prompt: In world-systems terms, dependency theory describes wealth and power flowing in which direction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: From less powerful nations to more powerful, wealthy nations

choices.label: B
choices.text: From wealthy nations down to poor nations equally

hints.rationale: Dependency theory says resources flow from weaker to more powerful nations.

hints.prompt: In this framework, which type of nation is the wealthy, powerful one that benefits from exploitation?
hints.rationale: Core nations are the wealthy, powerful nations that benefit from the arrangement.

hints.prompt: Dependency theory says the powerful exploit the weak. Which two options can be eliminated because they reverse this, claiming weaker nations exploit core nations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'semi-peripheral nations exploit core nations' and 'peripheral nations exploit core nations'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'core and peripheral exploit semi-peripheral' and 'core exploit peripheral'

hints.rationale: Both describe weaker nations exploiting the more powerful core, which reverses dependency theory's logic.

questions.stem: Dependency theorists explain global inequality and global stratification by focusing on the way that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: core nations and peripheral nations exploit semi-peripheral nations

questions.explanation: Dependency theory holds that global inequality results from core (wealthy) nations exploiting peripheral (poor) nations.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 10 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 10 Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: In sociology, how are 'sex' and 'gender' distinguished?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sex refers to biological or physical traits; gender refers to socially constructed roles and identities

choices.label: B
choices.text: They mean exactly the same thing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sex is learned while gender is biological

hints.rationale: Sex is biological while gender is the socially constructed set of roles and identities.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is an example of a BIOLOGICAL (sex) characteristic rather than a social (gender) one?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Following social expectations for how men should act

hints.rationale: Reproductive anatomy is biological, whereas 'masculine' and 'feminine' describe social gender.

hints.prompt: Given that 'masculine' and 'feminine' clearly refer to socially defined traits, which two options are the weakest answers?

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'both sex and gender' and 'none of the above'

hints.rationale: The terms are not biological, ruling out 'sex,' and they clearly refer to something, ruling out 'none of the above.'

questions.stem: The terms "masculine" and "feminine" refer to a person's _________.
questions.explanation: Masculine and feminine describe gender (socially constructed roles and identities), as distinguished from biological sex.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 12 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q1 = B.

hints.prompt: This item concerns the life stage when people typically become aware of their sexual orientation. 'Adolescence' refers to which period of life?
hints.rationale: Adolescence is the teenage period around puberty.

hints.prompt: The source ties awareness of sexual orientation to a developmental stage, not to birth. Why is 'at infancy' an implausible answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Infants have not yet developed the self-awareness the item describes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infancy is the same stage as adolescence

choices.label: D
choices.text: Infants are the main research subjects

hints.rationale: Infants lack the developed self-awareness the item describes, so 'at infancy' is implausible.

hints.prompt: The source points to a relatively early but post-childhood stage. Which two options are the furthest from that, one far too early and one far too late?
hints.rationale: Infancy is far too early and late adulthood far too late relative to the finding the item describes.

questions.stem: Research indicates that individuals are aware of their sexual orientation _______.
questions.explanation: Per the source, research indicates that people typically become aware of their sexual orientation in early adolescence.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 12 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: In sociology, an 'agent of socialization' is best described as what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A group or institution that teaches and reinforces social norms

choices.label: B
choices.text: A person who breaks all social rules

choices.label: C
choices.text: A government office that licenses adults

hints.rationale: Agents of socialization teach and reinforce social norms.

hints.prompt: Research generally finds that peer groups tend to do what regarding gender norms among school-aged children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pressure children to conform to gender norms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Have no effect on gender norms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encourage children to ignore all norms

hints.rationale: Peer groups typically pressure children to conform to gender norms.

hints.prompt: Which two options are weakest, one because it falsely claims children do not know gender roles and the other because it dismisses every choice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the option that children are 'unaware of gender roles' and 'None of the above'

choices.label: B
choices.text: the 'criticizing and marginalizing' option and 'None of the above'

choices.label: C
choices.text: the 'support system' option and the 'unaware of gender roles' option

choices.label: D
choices.text: the 'support system' option and the 'criticizing and marginalizing' option

hints.rationale: School-aged children clearly know gender roles, and a strong example does exist, so both of these are weak.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the role peers play as an agent of socialization for school-aged children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Children can act however they wish around their peers because children are unaware of gender roles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Peers serve as a support system for children who wish to act outside of their assigned gender roles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peers tend to reinforce gender roles by criticizing and marginalizing those who behave outside of their assigned roles.

questions.explanation: Peers are powerful agents of gender socialization, often reinforcing gender roles by criticizing or marginalizing children who deviate from them.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 12 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q5 = C.

hints.prompt: Broadly, the word 'misogyny' names a form of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prejudice or hatred directed at a group of people

hints.rationale: Misogyny names a form of prejudice or hatred toward a group.

hints.prompt: Which feature must the correct definition of 'misogyny' include, distinguishing it from the other options?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A negative attitude or prejudice toward a group of people

choices.label: D
choices.text: A process of learning social norms

hints.rationale: Misogyny is defined by a hostile attitude or prejudice, unlike a spa treatment, a biological trait, or a learning process.

hints.prompt: Misogyny is a hostile attitude toward a group. Which two options can be eliminated because they do not describe an attitude at all?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'a certain kind of spa treatment' and 'one's biological sex'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'the hatred of or prejudice against women' and 'a spa treatment'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'how we know our gender roles' and 'the hatred of women'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'one's biological sex' and 'the hatred of women'

hints.rationale: A spa treatment and a biological sex are not attitudes, so neither can define misogyny.

choices.label: A
choices.text: A certain kind if spa treatment

choices.label: C
choices.text: How we know our gender roles

choices.label: D
choices.text: the hatred of or, aversion to, or prejudice against women

questions.explanation: Misogyny is the hatred of, aversion to, or prejudice against women.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 12 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax (option A printed with typo 'if' for 'of'). correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: The idea that a 'division of labor' can produce inequality suggests that dividing work between groups can lead to differences in what?
hints.rationale: An unequal division of labor can create differences in power and resources.

hints.prompt: A perspective explaining inequality as arising from competition over power and resources is contrasted with one that instead sees the division of labor as helping society run smoothly. That stability-focused perspective is called what?
hints.rationale: Functionalism views the division of labor as serving social stability, unlike a focus on inequality and power.

hints.prompt: Which two perspectives are least focused on inequality arising from an unequal division of labor, one because it studies micro-level meanings and the other because it emphasizes social stability?
hints.rationale: Symbolic interactionism examines micro-level meanings and functionalism stresses stability, so neither centers on inequality from the division of labor.

questions.stem: The idea that gender inequality comes from the division of labor fits with which Sociological theory?
questions.explanation: Per the source, the view that gender inequality arises from the division of labor is keyed to conflict theory, which emphasizes how divisions of labor produce unequal power and resources.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 12 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q11 = C.

hints.prompt: This item measures women's share of elected officials. If women were represented exactly in proportion to their share of the population, that figure would be closest to what?
hints.rationale: Women are roughly half the population, so proportional representation would be near 50%.

hints.prompt: The item highlights women's UNDER-representation in Congress. That implies the actual percentage is which, relative to 50%?
hints.rationale: Underrepresentation means the actual share is well below the roughly 50% parity mark.

hints.prompt: Given clear underrepresentation, which two percentages are too high to fit because they approach or reach parity?
hints.rationale: 30.4% and 50% approach or reach parity, which is inconsistent with strong underrepresentation.

questions.stem: In the 115th Congress of the United States, what percentage of the elected officials were women?
questions.explanation: Per the source, women made up about 21.2% of the elected officials in the 115th U.S. Congress, illustrating the underrepresentation of women in political institutions.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 12 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q13 = B.

hints.prompt: This item compares U.S. sexual attitudes to those of other Western societies. Describing attitudes as 'conservative' generally means what?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Attitudes identical to every other nation

hints.rationale: Here 'conservative' means comparatively restrictive or traditional.

hints.prompt: The words 'liberal,' 'permissive,' and 'free' all point in the same general direction regarding sexual attitudes. Which direction is that?
hints.rationale: Liberal, permissive, and free all describe greater openness, the opposite of restrictive.

hints.prompt: Three of the options mean roughly the same thing, so none of them can be the single correct answer. Which two are clearly interchangeable synonyms that can both be eliminated?
hints.rationale: 'Liberal' and 'permissive' are near-synonyms, so neither can be the unique correct answer.

questions.stem: Compared to most Western societies, U.S. sexual attitudes are considered _______.
questions.explanation: Compared with most other Western societies, U.S. sexual attitudes are relatively conservative.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 12 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q15 = A.
